figures.gloss: The Lady of the high peaks who dwells in caves and crosses the sky as a sickle of fire, combing her hair of gold; she gathers the weather, punishes the liar and the thief, and is the supreme power of the old Basque world.
figures.facet: A goddess who is mountain, weather and earth at once with no male sky-god above her — the rare shard where the great-mother is herself the sovereign, not the consort, and where moral order is enforced by a woman of the caves rather than a thundering father.
figures.provenance: Reconstructed largely from José Miguel de Barandiarán's early-20th-c. field ethnography of surviving oral tradition; the pre-Christian theology is recovered, not textually attested.

figures.gloss: The male serpent or dragon, Mari's consort, who moves through the sky as lightning or a fiery crescent; their meeting in the caves is said to brew the storms.
figures.facet: The serpent here is not the dragon to be slain but the husband of the sovereign goddess — a cosmic snake folded into the world-parents pairing rather than cast as the adversary of order.
figures.provenance: Sparsely attested; chiefly from Barandiarán's collections, where Sugaar shades into the dragon Herensuge.

figures.gloss: The personified sky or firmament, surviving mostly fossilised in Basque words for the heavens, weather and the days of the week (ortzegun, ortzadar — the rainbow as 'sky-bow').
figures.facet: A sky-god attested almost only in the bones of the language — a name that may have been more grammar than god, the faintest possible sky-father, recoverable but never enthroned.
figures.provenance: Contested: a 12th-c. pilgrim's gloss ('Urcia' for God) is the chief external witness; many scholars doubt Ortzi was ever a fully personified deity rather than a word for 'sky/weather'.

figures.gloss: The great seven-headed serpent-dragon of the caves and gorges, devourer of livestock and maidens, slain in legend by the hero or by Saint Michael / Saint Theodosius.
figures.facet: In the same tradition the serpent is BOTH consort-of-the-goddess (Sugaar) and slain monster (Herensuge) — the shard preserves the cosmic-serpent at the very moment Christianity is rewriting it into the dragon to be killed.
figures.provenance: Later legend cycles, heavily Christianised in transmission.

figures.gloss: River- and spring-dwelling women with the feet of birds or webbed feet, who comb their hair with golden combs by the water and build bridges and dolmens by night in exchange for offerings.
figures.facet: Nature-spirits of the waters who must be paid in bread and cider, not worshipped — the sacred as reciprocal neighbour rather than transcendent power.
figures.provenance: Widely attested in Basque folklore; Barandiarán and folk collections.

figures.gloss: The 'Lord of the Woods,' a tall shaggy wild man of the forests who guards the flocks and from whom humankind stole the secrets of agriculture, the forge, and the saw.
figures.facet: The culture-hero inverted: civilisation is not gifted by a benefactor but STOLEN from the wild man of the woods — the arts of grain and iron taken from nature rather than given by a god.
figures.provenance: Folk legend, esp. the San Martin Txiki cycle.

figures.gloss: A genius of storm and fire whose passage across the sky brings tempest, hail and flood; his coming is announced so the people may take shelter.
figures.facet: A storm-power that is a portent to be read rather than a god to be petitioned — weather as the surface on which Mari's order becomes legible.
figures.provenance: Thinly attested; from Barandiarán's regional collections, with significant local variation.

figures.gloss: The race of giants who lived before the Christians, builders of the dolmens and standing stones, who fled or died when the shining sign (Kixmi / Christ) appeared in the sky.
figures.facet: A people's own myth of its predecessors: the megaliths explained as the work of a vanished elder race that the new faith extinguished — a tradition narrating its own supersession in stone.
figures.provenance: Jentilak / Olentzero legend cycle; the 'death of the Jentilak' explicitly marks the arrival of Christianity.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Wild, physically distinct lords of the forest who protect nature from human overreach.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A massive indigenous dragon that terrorizes the physical environment and is central to pre-colonial myth.
The sovereign Lady and her serpent-consort: their union in the caves brews the storms — a world-parents pairing where, uniquely, the goddess outranks the snake.
Both rest on Basque 'suge' (serpent); in the legends Sugaar and the dragon Herensuge bleed into one another — the consort-serpent being rewritten into the slain monster under Christianisation.
The same serpent that is Mari's husband becomes the dragon slain by Saint Michael — within one tradition the cosmic-serpent and the chaos-dragon split apart.
Earth-and-sovereignty as a single female power; but Mari is mountain-and-weather and morally enforcing, where Gaia is the generative ground beneath the gods.
A sovereign female power of the land tied to weather, omen and the punishment of oath-breakers — independent witnesses to the moral-sovereign goddess.
The goddess who crosses the sky as fire — solar/luminous sovereignty held by a female, against the more common male sun; a reach across utterly unrelated traditions.
The serpent as benign cosmic principle rather than monster — feathered or fiery, consort to creation rather than its enemy.
Both feed the late hagiographic dragon-slaying frame (Michael, the saint vs. the serpent); offered as structural parallel only, heavily Christian-mediated.
A sky-word that may once have named a sky-god — but Basque is a NON-IE isolate, so any echo of Dyeus is areal/typological resemblance, NOT descent; a deliberately low tier.
Both shards of a faded sky-father — Tyr demoted by sound-law cognate to Dyeus, Ortzi surviving only as grammar; the parallel is in the FADING, not in shared origin.
The golden comb links them — Mari combs her hair of gold on the peaks, the lamiak theirs by the water; the same image distributed from sovereign to nature-spirit.
Prometheus steals fire FROM the gods FOR mankind; humankind steals agriculture and the forge FROM Basajaun — the culture-gift reversed, taken from the wild not granted from above.
The forest-lord as origin of the forge and iron-craft — the wild source of metallurgy, paralleling the orisa of iron and the smith-archetype.
Both are the elder, pre-human or pre-Christian inhabitants of the wild — the Jentilak as megalith-builders, Basajaun as the woodland ancestor of the arts.
A storm-power crossing the sky with thunder and hail — but Eate is read as a portent to shelter from, not a champion-god to invoke.
Both punish the lie, the false oath and the proud — moral order enforced by the supreme power; offered only as a structural echo across a vast gulf.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent secretive, parallel human-like races living in the wilderness who occasionally aid, guide, or protect humans who respect their territory.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as wild, hairy lords of the forest who protect the wilderness and represent raw natural vitality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the deeply territorial, wild protector of the deep forest who governs the boundary between human expansion and untamed nature.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both survived Christianization as powerful, untamed mountain spirits—Thana as the Albanian Zana, and Mari in the Pyrenees.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Beautiful, territorial female nature spirits who seduce men and ruthlessly punish transgressors against their localized moral codes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A formidable, elemental female sovereign who presides over ecstatic, nocturnal gatherings of witches and spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The powerful, untamed master of the deep forest who possesses intimate knowledge of the land, protecting it while sharing ancient survival wisdom.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The wild, territorial lord of the deep forest who exists completely outside human communal order.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Basques, speakers of Europe's only surviving language isolate, in the western Pyrenees. No ancient text describes it; it is reconstructed from Aquitanian votive inscriptions of the Roman era and, above all, from folklore collected in the 19th-20th centuries (chiefly by Jose Miguel de Barandiaran), which preserves a system centered on the cave-dwelling sovereign goddess Mari, a landscape of reciprocal spirits, and a household-anchored cult of the dead. Christianization was slow and layered rather than a datable event.
dossier.cosmos: Orientation is chthonic rather than celestial: divinity dwells inside the earth — Mari in the high caves, crossing the sky between peaks as fire — and in the folk cosmology recorded by Barandiaran the sun and moon are daughters of the earth (Amalur) who return into her each night, making the underground the true otherworld. Storms brew from Mari's meetings with her serpent consort Sugaar, and her justice punishes the lie, the theft, and the boast. The dead remain attached to the house (etxe), receiving light and offerings; no developed heaven, hell, or judgment survives in the folk record.
dossier.practice: No priesthood is attested: practice was domestic and reciprocal — offerings of food left for the lamiak in exchange for their night-work, apotropaic emblems such as the eguzkilore (a dried sun-thistle) fixed on doors, and the mistress of the house (etxekoandre) officiating for the household dead, in later centuries with argizaiola wax-boards burned at family tombs. Legend remembers petition of Mari at her caves for weather and redress; the akelarre ('he-goat meadow') witch-gatherings prosecuted at Zugarramurdi (1609-1614) show older nocturnal assemblies as the Inquisition refracted them.
dossier.canon: None: the corpus is oral folklore — legends, house-rites, and place-traditions — collected principally in Barandiaran's Eusko-Folklore materials and synthesized in his 'Mitologia vasca' (1960).
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds sovereignty relocated — supreme power gendered female and housed in the earth, with religion practiced as neighborly reciprocity rather than temple cult.
sources: Julio Caro Baroja, 'The World of the Witches' (1964, English translation)
'Selected Writings of Jose Miguel de Barandiaran: Basque Prehistory and Ethnography' (2007)
